3. Each negation must rule out a *plausible* misunderstanding — not straw men.
4. After 4-6 negations, the Speaker must deliver a single affirmative sentence that says what it *is.* This sentence should feel like a revelation after the negations.
5. Six concepts per round.

**Suggested concepts:** justice, intelligence, leadership, beauty, freedom, maturity, integrity, wisdom.
exercises.good: Each negation makes the audience rethink their assumptions. The negations aren't obvious — they rule out things the audience might actually have believed. The final affirmative definition lands with force precisely because of the clearing work the negations did. The overall effect is: "I didn't know what this was, but now I do — and I understand it better because I first saw everything it's not."

sets.title: Integration and Mastery
sets.intro: These exercises combine techniques from all previous sets. They test whether the speaker can deploy specific techniques on demand, combine multiple techniques in a single passage, and select the right technique for a given rhetorical situation. This is where practice becomes craft.

exercises.title: Rhetoric Roulette
exercises.capability: Deploying any named technique within seconds.
exercises.why: Knowing what anaphora is and being able to produce it on demand under time pressure are entirely different things. This exercise tests whether the techniques from Sets 1-9 have become available tools, not just concepts.
exercises.setup: Speaker is mid-flow on a topic. Director calls out technique names.
exercises.signals:
- Director calls a technique name every 15 seconds.
- **Thumbs up** = Correct technique, well-executed.
- **Flat hand** = Correct technique, but weak execution.
- **Thumbs down** = Wrong technique.
exercises.rules:
1. Speaker begins on any topic.
2. Director calls: "Anaphora." Speaker must immediately deploy anaphora — starting successive clauses with the same phrase.
3. Director calls: "Chiasmus." Speaker must produce a chiasmus.
4. Director calls: "Asyndeton." Speaker drops all conjunctions.
5. The topic remains continuous — each technique must serve the argument, not interrupt it.
6. Ten techniques per round.

**Technique menu:** anaphora, epistrophe, anadiplosis, tricolon, antithesis, chiasmus, asyndeton, polysyndeton, reductio, a fortiori, concession-pivot, metaphor, personification, periodic sentence, cumulative sentence, via negativa, zeugma.
exercises.good: The speaker deploys each technique within 3 seconds of hearing its name. The technique is correctly executed — not just attempted. The topic continues to develop; the techniques serve it rather than derailing it. The overall passage, despite the rapid technique changes, is actually a coherent argument.

exercises.title: The Layered Response
exercises.capability: Combining multiple rhetorical techniques in a single passage.
exercises.why: In real rhetoric, techniques don't appear one at a time. The best passages combine metaphor with antithesis, tricolon with anaphora, concession with periodic structure. This exercise trains the speaker to layer techniques — using each one to amplify the others.
exercises.setup: Director specifies three techniques. Speaker must combine all three in a single passage.
exercises.signals:
- Director names three techniques before each round.
- After the passage, Director identifies which techniques they spotted.
- **Score** = number of specified techniques successfully deployed.
exercises.rules:
1. Director names three techniques: "Antithesis + tricolon + metaphor."
2. Speaker has 60 seconds to deliver a passage on any topic that deploys all three.
3. Example: "A nation is a garden. You can plant it in hope and harvest it in despair; you can water it with attention and starve it with neglect; you can grow it for a generation or destroy it in a season." (Metaphor: garden. Antithesis: each tricolon element contains an opposition. Tricolon: three parallel clauses.)
4. The passage must sound *natural* — not like a demonstration of technique. The audience should feel the effect without seeing the machinery.
5. Director scores. Six combinations per round.

**Suggested combinations:**
- Anaphora + metaphor + periodic sentence
- Concession + antithesis + short sentence close
- Polysyndeton + personification + a fortiori
- Chiasmus + cumulative sentence + emotional coloring
exercises.good: All three techniques are clearly present, but the passage doesn't feel forced or mechanical. The techniques amplify each other — the antithesis gives the tricolon its edge, the metaphor gives the antithesis its image. The passage would be impressive to someone who couldn't name any of the techniques.

exercises.title: The Shapeshifter
exercises.capability: Transitioning between techniques mid-sentence without losing coherence.
exercises.why: This tests the deepest level of rhetorical fluency — the ability to shift between modes of expression as naturally as a musician shifts between keys. The speaker who can shapeshifter has techniques available not as set pieces but as real-time options.
exercises.setup: Speaker is mid-flow. Director signals technique changes.
exercises.signals:
- Director signals by holding up a card, a hand signal, or calling the technique softly.
- The transition must happen within the *current* sentence or the very next one.
exercises.rules:
1. Speaker begins on a topic using any technique.
2. Director signals a new technique. Speaker must transition to it within one sentence. No pausing, no "okay, now..."
3. The transition must be *invisible* — an audience member who didn't see the signal shouldn't notice a break.
4. If the Speaker has to stop and restart, the attempt doesn't count.
5. Eight transitions per round.

**Advanced variant.** Director signals at increasing speed — eventually one technique every 5 seconds.
exercises.good: The transitions are seamless. The audience experiences a shifting, dynamic passage that feels organic, not interrupted. The speaker moves from anaphora to metaphor to antithesis like water flowing around stones — no pause, no announcement, no visible effort.

exercises.title: The Debate Decathlon
exercises.capability: Selecting and executing the right technique for each rhetorical situation.
exercises.why: All previous exercises tell you *which* technique to use. This one doesn't. Half the skill is choosing the right tool for the moment — reductio when the opponent's logic is vulnerable, concession when they're strongest, metaphor when the concept is abstract, antithesis when the contrast is clear. This exercise tests rhetorical judgment, not just execution.
exercises.setup: Director gives ten rapid prompts. Speaker chooses and deploys the best technique for each.
exercises.signals:
- Director gives a prompt every 30 seconds: a question, a claim, a scenario, a challenge.
- After each, Director names the technique the Speaker used and rates how well-matched it was to the situation.
exercises.rules:
1. Director gives a prompt: "Your opponent says technology makes us less human."
2. Speaker has 30 seconds to respond using whatever technique they think is most effective. They do not announce the technique.
3. After the response, Director identifies the technique and evaluates: Was it the right tool? Was it well-executed?
4. Score on two dimensions: **Selection** (1-5: was this the best technique for this prompt?) and **Execution** (1-5: was it well-done?).
5. Ten prompts per round.

**Suggested prompt types:** a strong opposing argument (steelman + demolish), an absurd claim (reductio), a complex abstraction (metaphor), an emotional appeal that needs countering (concession + pivot), a request to explain something simply (analogy), a need for decisive closing (tricolon + short sentence).
exercises.good: The speaker instinctively reaches for the right technique. Reductio against overextended logic, not against a strong point. Concession against a strong point, not against a weak one. Metaphor for abstraction, antithesis for contrast. The selection feels as skilled as the execution.

exercises.title: The Masterclass Minute
exercises.capability: Improvised rhetorical virtuosity.
exercises.why: This is the final exam. Sixty seconds, no interruptions, no signals, no constraints. Just the speaker, a topic, and everything they've learned. The goal is not to cram in as many techniques as possible — it's to deliver a passage so compelling that the audience forgets they're watching an exercise.
exercises.setup: Speaker delivers 60 seconds of uninterrupted speech. Director tallies techniques afterward.
exercises.signals:
None during the passage. Afterward:
- Director names every technique they spotted.
- Director rates the passage holistically: 1-5.
exercises.rules:
1. Director gives a topic. Speaker has 10 seconds to think.
2. Speaker delivers 60 seconds of uninterrupted speech.
3. No constraints, no signals, no required techniques. The speaker deploys whatever serves the topic and the moment.
4. After the minute, Director identifies every technique used and rates the overall passage.
5. Scoring:
   - **Technique count** — how many distinct techniques were deployed
   - **Coherence** (1-5) — did it hold together as a single argument/narrative?
   - **Impact** (1-5) — would a non-expert audience have been moved?
   - **Naturalness** (1-5) — did the techniques feel organic or displayed?
6. Three topics per round. Aim for improvement across the three.
exercises.good: The passage is genuinely compelling. It has structure (setup/development/payoff). It uses figurative language that illuminates. It has rhythmic variety that serves the content. It builds to a close that lands. The techniques are invisible — present everywhere but visible nowhere. An audience member who knows nothing about rhetoric feels moved. An audience member who knows everything about rhetoric is impressed. This is what fluency sounds like.

techniques.name: Sentence length control
techniques.definition: Varying sentence length for rhythm and emphasis

techniques.name: Saxon/Latinate diction
techniques.definition: Toggling between concrete Anglo-Saxon and abstract Latinate vocabulary

techniques.name: Cumulative sentence
techniques.definition: Main clause first, then modifying phrases added

techniques.name: Periodic sentence
techniques.definition: Subordinate clauses first, main clause withheld until the end

techniques.name: Anaphora
techniques.definition: Repeating words at the beginning of successive clauses

techniques.name: Epistrophe
techniques.definition: Repeating words at the end of successive clauses

techniques.name: Anadiplosis
techniques.definition: Ending a clause with a word that begins the next

techniques.name: Tricolon
techniques.definition: Three parallel elements of similar length and structure

techniques.name: Polyptoton
techniques.definition: Using different forms of the same root word

techniques.name: Antithesis
techniques.definition: Juxtaposing contrasting ideas in balanced structure

techniques.name: Chiasmus
techniques.definition: ABBA reversal pattern across two phrases

techniques.name: Concession-pivot
techniques.definition: Genuinely conceding the opponent's point, then recontextualizing

techniques.name: Dilemma
techniques.definition: Presenting two options, both with serious consequences

techniques.name: Metaphor
techniques.definition: Mapping one domain onto another to illuminate structure

techniques.name: Extended metaphor
techniques.definition: Sustaining a single metaphor over a long passage

techniques.name: Personification
techniques.definition: Giving abstract concepts character and agency

techniques.name: Analogy
techniques.definition: Drawing structural parallels between unlike things

techniques.name: Asyndeton
techniques.definition: Omitting conjunctions for speed and force

techniques.name: Polysyndeton
techniques.definition: Multiplying conjunctions for weight and accumulation

techniques.name: Isocolon
techniques.definition: Parallel phrases of matching length and structure

techniques.name: Reductio ad absurdum
techniques.definition: Extending an opponent's logic to absurdity

techniques.name: A fortiori
techniques.definition: Arguing from a lesser to a greater case

techniques.name: Distinguishing cases
techniques.definition: Showing why an apparent precedent doesn't apply

techniques.name: Steelman
techniques.definition: Building the strongest version of the opposing argument

techniques.name: Tu quoque
techniques.definition: Turning an argument back on the one making it

techniques.name: Register shift
techniques.definition: Moving between formal, conversational, and colloquial levels

techniques.name: Emotional coloring
techniques.definition: Changing emotional register without changing content

techniques.name: Distance control
techniques.definition: Shifting between intimate/personal and analytical/detached

techniques.name: Ethopoeia
techniques.definition: Speaking in another's characteristic rhetorical style

techniques.name: In medias res
techniques.definition: Beginning at the dramatic moment, then providing context

techniques.name: Nested digression
techniques.definition: Departing from and returning to the main thread

techniques.name: Three-movement structure
techniques.definition: Setup, development, payoff

techniques.name: Ring composition
techniques.definition: Returning to the opening image/phrase, now transformed

techniques.name: Compression
techniques.definition: Progressive distillation to essential argument

techniques.name: Ellipsis
techniques.definition: Strategic omission of words for strength

techniques.name: Aphorism
techniques.definition: Compressing insight into a single memorable sentence

techniques.name: Zeugma
techniques.definition: One word governing two objects from different domains

techniques.name: Via negativa
techniques.definition: Defining by what something is not
